head.hi: The Ambassador in Italy (Dunn) to the Secretary of State

div.p:
5309. FonOff explains Sforza felt obliged keep treaty revision initiative and since no public official reaction from 3 powers to his letter to Schuman (Embtel 4704 Apr 17Document 269.) he felt it necessary in Genoa speechOn May 20 Sforza gave a speech in Genoa in which he asked for the annulment of the military and certain other clauses of the Treaty of Peace with Italy and the admission of Italy to the United Nations. The speech is printed, in part, in Folliot, Documents on International Affairs, 1951, p. 78. to restate in specific terms Ital Govt’s position.
While he realizes Paris deputies negots complicate situation, FonOff says that Sforza considers in those very negots 3 Western powers might adopt a position with respect Ital peace treaty somewhat in line his Genoa suggestions. Evidently FonMin feels situation is morally intolerable wherein Italy, former co-belligerent and now ally, is more bound by peace treaty restrictions than any of former enemy countries, including both present occupation status, and anticipated peace treaty status of Germany and Japan.

ReEmbtel 5211, May 18,Telegram 5211 reported that an official of the Italian Foreign Ministry informed the Embassy that the Italian Government intended to request early action by the Western Powers to express agreement in a joint statement that the Italian Peace Treaty needed revision in order to eliminate its onerous provisions. (665.001/5–1851) FonOff does not anticipate further official representations at present time. Emb Wash has been instructed explain to McDermott, in connection his statement, May 21This is presumably a reference to a statement made by Michael J. McDermott, Press Secretary of the Department of State, during the daily noon briefing on May 21. that while true Ital Govt officially communicated views treaty revision to US Govt, still Sforza’s statement of Ital position in Genoa speech is official position Ital Govt for all to take note of.

In our opinion Sforza felt obliged resume initiative peace treaty revision in Genoa speech in order establish clear record for govt of working actively for removal moral stigma of “diktat”. For purposes imminent elections revision question appears to us to be primarily internal issue, i.e., reinforcing govt’s record of pursuing energetic, independent, Italian foreign policy; and on other hand refuting opposition charges govt, especially FonMin, slavishly fol along behind Western allies, particularly US. So far at any rate question has not involved issue foreign Ital relations nor, specifically, active Western support Ital aspirations. Undoubtedly that aspect will appear eventually but not yet apparent.

We do not believe electoral situation requires any positive tripartite support or acceptance Sforza’s proposal to election; although there are doubtless eager elements in govt who see in situation opportunity to press US for commitment.

In our view requirements of election campaign can be considered met by Sec’s statement May 18For information concerning Acheson’s statement of May 18, see the memorandum of conversation, supra. and McDermott’s comment May 21 which well played in Ital press (Embtel 5300 May 23Telegram 5300 summarized the reaction of the non-Communist Italian newspapers to Sforza’s speech. (665.001/5–2351)). Pub and govt seem appreciate our position of reluctance take definite stand on issue during Paris negot. Of course, it wld be highly embarrassing and politically damaging to govt if we shld give any pub indication
of opposing govt’s position as expressed by FonMin. We believe that some positive action on the treaty cannot long be delayed, and will submit comments and suggestions shortly.
Official translation full text being made available today will be airmailed soonest.
